name: neo4j
description: Run Cypher queries against a Neo4j Aura or self-hosted graph database via the Query API for graph data and GraphRAG workloads

envVars.name: NEO4J_USERNAME
envVars.description: Neo4j database username (Aura instances default to 'neo4j'), used as the HTTP Basic username

envVars.name: NEO4J_PASSWORD
envVars.description: Neo4j database password (shown once when an Aura instance is created), used as the HTTP Basic password

envVars.name: NEO4J_HOST
envVars.description: Neo4j host serving the Query API, e.g. xxxxxxxx.databases.neo4j.io for Aura or your-server:7474 for self-hosted — pass it as the host parameter of each tool

tools.name: Run Cypher Query
tools.description: Execute a read-only Cypher query (MATCH ... RETURN) in an implicit transaction and get back fields and result rows — use for lookups, traversals, and GraphRAG retrieval. Note: the Query API does not enforce read-only access, so only submit read statements here (use a read-only database user to guarantee no writes)

host.description: Neo4j host, e.g. xxxxxxxx.databases.neo4j.io for Aura (HTTPS on port 443) or your self-hosted server host

databaseName.description: Database to query (Aura and default installs use 'neo4j')

statement.description: Cypher statement to execute, e.g. MATCH (n:Person {name: $name})-[:KNOWS]->(m) RETURN m.name — use $placeholders with the parameters field instead of inlining values

parameters.description: Values for $placeholders in the statement, e.g. { "name": "Alice" }

tools.name: Run Cypher Write
tools.description: Execute a Cypher statement that modifies the graph (CREATE, MERGE, SET, DELETE) in an implicit transaction

host.description: Neo4j host, e.g. xxxxxxxx.databases.neo4j.io for Aura (HTTPS on port 443) or your self-hosted server host

databaseName.description: Database to write to (Aura and default installs use 'neo4j')

statement.description: Cypher write statement, e.g. MERGE (n:Person {name: $name, age: $age}) RETURN n — use $placeholders with the parameters field instead of inlining values

parameters.description: Values for $placeholders in the statement, e.g. { "name": "Alice", "age": 42 }

includeCounters.description: Set true to include counters (nodes/relationships created, properties set) in the response

prompts.title: Explore the graph
prompts.prompt: Query my Neo4j database to show the node labels, relationship types, and a few sample nodes so I understand the graph model.

prompts.title: Answer from the graph
prompts.prompt: Answer my question by writing and running a parameterized Cypher query against my Neo4j database, then explain the result.

SETUP_GUIDE.title: Neo4j Query API Setup

steps.title: Create a Neo4j Aura instance
steps.description: Sign up at https://console.neo4j.io and create an AuraDB Free instance (no credit card required). Download or copy the generated password — it is shown only once. Self-hosted Neo4j 5.19+ also exposes the Query API.

steps.title: Find your host
steps.description: From the Aura console, copy the instance's connection URI (neo4j+s://xxxxxxxx.databases.neo4j.io). The host part (xxxxxxxx.databases.neo4j.io) is what tools take as the host parameter; store it in NEO4J_HOST for reference.

steps.title: Store credentials
steps.description: Add NEO4J_USERNAME=neo4j and NEO4J_PASSWORD=... to your .env file. The Query API authenticates with HTTP Basic auth using these values.

steps.title: Verify access
steps.description: Run the Run Cypher Query tool with statement 'RETURN 1 AS ok' against database 'neo4j'. A 401 means wrong credentials; a connection error usually means the wrong host.

notes: The Query API requires Neo4j 5.19+ (all Aura instances qualify); on self-managed instances below 5.25 it is disabled by default and must be enabled in the server configuration. Aura serves it over HTTPS on port 443
Self-hosted instances must expose HTTPS (port 7473 by default) for these tools, since tool URLs use https:// — include the port in the host parameter, e.g. my-server:7473
Always pass Cypher values via the parameters object ($placeholders) rather than string-concatenating them into the statement — this enables plan caching and prevents Cypher injection
Both tools run implicit (auto-commit) transactions; explicit multi-request transactions are not exposed

The Query API has no server-side read-only mode: Run Cypher Query is read-only by convention only, and write statements sent through it would execute. Connect with a read-only database user if you need a hard guarantee
